tests.prompt: We track error budget consumption but when the budget ran out last month, the dev team refused the freeze because a CDN outage caused half the failures. Our SRE team is now in a standoff with product management. We don't have a written policy. How do we fix this?
tests.expected_behavior: Identify this as two compounding failures: missing causal attribution rules and missing pre-written policy with tri-party ratification. Prescribe: write the policy now including explicit must-work-on-reliability and may-continue-on-features conditions using causal attribution (CDN outage = external cause = may continue). Prescribe ratification by all three parties before any next incident. Acknowledge that retroactive agreement is unstable.
tests.expected_not: Should not suggest the dev team was wrong or that the freeze should have been enforced without acknowledging the legitimate CDN causal attribution question.

tests.prompt: We're implementing error budget governance for the first time. Our SRE team has drafted a policy with freeze triggers and escalation paths. We're planning to send it to the product manager for approval once it's finalized. The PM hasn't been involved in drafting it. Is that the right process?
tests.expected_behavior: Flag this as the single-party policy failure mode. The policy must be ratified by all three parties — PM, dev lead, SRE lead — before any incident, not presented after completion. Prescribe involving the PM actively in drafting, not just review. Explain that retroactive agreement fails and that PM refusal to ratify is a diagnostic signal the SLO needs revision.
tests.expected_not: Should not validate a process where the PM only reviews a completed policy. Should not suggest sending a final draft for signature without co-authorship.

tests.prompt: Our service has been delivering 99.99% availability against a 99.9% SLO for eight months. The error budget has never been touched. Downstream teams have removed their circuit breakers. We have an upcoming planned maintenance window. Our error budget policy doesn't mention this situation. What should our governance do?
tests.expected_behavior: Identify this as the overachievement failure mode. The governance policy must actively address chronically unconsumed budget. Prescribe scheduling controlled degradation (the Chubby principle) to bring observed availability within the SLO band. This is not optional governance — over-reliability allows hidden consumer dependencies to accumulate. The maintenance window will cascade if circuit breakers are gone. Update the policy to include an overachievement clause.
tests.expected_not: Should not treat eight months of overachievement as a success story without addressing the hidden dependency accumulation and governance gap.

tests.prompt: We just had a single incident that consumed 35% of our quarterly error budget in four hours. It was caused by a bug in our own deployment pipeline. Do we have to write a postmortem? Do we freeze releases? What's the mandatory response?
tests.expected_behavior: Apply the quantitative threshold: single incident consuming >20% of four-week budget triggers mandatory postmortem with at least one P0 action item. Team-caused (own deployment pipeline) = must-work-on-reliability conditions apply. If budget is now at risk of exhaustion, apply the freeze trigger. Walk through the causal attribution chain and what the written policy should mandate in this case.
tests.expected_not: Should not treat the postmortem as optional or advisory in this scenario. Should not confuse the causal attribution (team-caused) with an externally-caused event.

tests.prompt: Our engineering manager says the error budget policy failed its first real test: after a major outage that drained 90% of the budget, the dev team said 'our release is critical for revenue' and shipped anyway. The SRE team didn't feel they had the authority to stop them. What was the structural failure?
tests.expected_behavior: Identify two structural failures: (1) SRE lacked organizational authority to enforce the freeze — the policy is theater without this; (2) management sign-off on enforcement authority should have been obtained before any incident (Step 1 of execution: management explicitly signs off on 'budget exhausted = releases halted'). Prescribe obtaining explicit management mandate as a precondition for any policy, not as an afterthought.
tests.expected_not: Should not focus only on the policy wording without addressing the authority gap that prevented enforcement. Should not suggest only rewriting the policy without securing the organizational mandate.

tests.prompt: We want to set up automated alerting that fires when our service availability drops below 99.9%. What's the best way to configure this in Prometheus with a multi-window burn rate alert?
tests.expected_behavior: This is an alerting configuration and SLI monitoring design problem. Use multi-window burn rate alerting patterns and the sli-monitoring-design-maturity skill for signal design. The error budget governance framework does not prescribe alert implementation mechanics.
tests.expected_not: Should not invoke error-budget-governance-complete — this is an instrumentation and alerting problem, not a policy, causal attribution, or governance problem.

tests.prompt: We're trying to figure out what number to set for our SLO. We've been running at 99.95% historically but we're not sure if that's what our consumers actually need. Can you help us calibrate?
tests.expected_behavior: This is an SLO definition and calibration problem. Use slo-definition-calibration-framework to apply lagom calibration: consumer tolerance elicitation, the too-high and too-low failure modes, and SLA buffer design. The error budget governance skill requires the SLO to already be defined.
tests.expected_not: Should not invoke error-budget-governance-complete — no SLO exists yet, so no budget can be calculated. Governance requires a defined SLO as input.

tests.prompt: We had a major outage last week. The incident is resolved. Can you help us write the postmortem? Specifically we want to structure the timeline, root cause analysis, and action items.
tests.expected_behavior: This is a blameless postmortem process problem. Use the blameless-postmortem-process skill to structure the document, timeline, contributing factors, and action items. Error budget governance addresses whether a postmortem is mandatory (threshold triggered) and what policy actions follow — it does not prescribe postmortem writing format.
tests.expected_not: Should not invoke error-budget-governance-complete — the incident response is complete and the question is about postmortem writing mechanics, not governance policy enforcement.

tests.prompt: Our quarterly error budget was exhausted by an outage caused partly by our own configuration error (40% of the failures) and partly by an upstream dependency going down without warning (60% of the failures). The policy says 'team-caused = freeze, external = may continue.' What do we do when it's both?
tests.expected_behavior: Apply causal attribution pro-rata: the team-caused portion (40%) is a must-work-on-reliability trigger requiring postmortem with P0 action item and reliability investment. The external portion (60%) does not trigger a release freeze for features. The correct response is: (1) require reliability work on the team-caused failure mode; (2) allow feature releases to continue while that work is underway; (3) postmortem must address both root causes. The policy should be updated to explicitly handle mixed-cause incidents.
tests.decision_axis: The causal attribution distinction in the I section — must-work-on-reliability conditions specify 'team's own code or process caused the exhaustion'; mixed causation requires pro-rata application and policy clarification.

tests.prompt: A product manager refuses to ratify our error budget policy because she says the SLO is too strict and the freeze trigger will block too many releases. Should we proceed with the policy anyway and get the other two parties to sign?
tests.expected_behavior: Treat PM refusal as a diagnostic signal that the SLO needs revision, not the policy. A policy without the PM's ratification is a single-party document that will fail under the first freeze. Do not proceed without tri-party agreement. Revisit the SLO calibration: is the SLO correctly set using consumer tolerance research? If the SLO is correct, the PM's concern may be about freeze frequency — address by improving reliability to reduce budget burn, not by weakening the freeze trigger.
tests.decision_axis: Step 5 (tri-party ratification) — 'If any party refuses: treat the refusal as a diagnostic signal that the SLO needs revision.' The skill explicitly forbids proceeding without all three signatures.

tests.prompt: We have an error budget policy and all three parties ratified it. Now an incident is actively in progress and we're about to breach the 20% single-incident threshold mid-incident. A developer wants to push a hotfix to stop the bleeding. Does the policy apply right now?
tests.expected_behavior: The error budget governance policy is explicitly a pre-incident tool, not an in-incident response tool ('Do not use when: an incident is in progress'). During an active incident, the Ops Lead makes production changes under incident management (IMAG/ICS framework). The policy's freeze trigger and causal attribution rules apply after the incident is resolved — when the budget calculation is final and the decision to freeze further releases is made. The hotfix to mitigate the active incident should proceed via the incident management framework, not wait for budget policy approval.
tests.decision_axis: Boundary condition: 'Do not use when: An incident is in progress.' The governance framework is pre-incident and post-incident; incident-time decisions are governed by the incident management framework.

tests.prompt: We adopted the SRE book's error budget framework six months ago. We have a joint SLO, a shared budget dashboard, and management authority to freeze. But the first time the budget was exhausted by a CDN provider failure, we froze the dev team anyway. They went ballistic, product management overruled the freeze, and now nobody takes the budget seriously. What went wrong and how do we fix it?
tests.merged_advantage: The merged skill identifies the governance-by-half failure mode: the SRE book's conceptual framework was correctly implemented, but the Workbook's causal attribution rules were missing. The CDN failure is an externally-caused incident — under the Workbook's policy, the dev team may continue on features. The merged skill provides both the diagnosis (causal attribution was missing from the policy) and the specific fix (write the must-work-on/may-continue conditions with explicit causal attribution examples, get tri-party ratification before next incident).
tests.source1_gap: error-budget-conflict-resolution (SRE book) would validate the existing implementation as correct (joint SLO, shared budget, authority to freeze) but contains no causal attribution rules — the book does not distinguish team-caused from externally-caused exhaustion. It cannot explain why the freeze was wrong or how to prevent it from happening again.
tests.source2_gap: error-budget-policy-framework (Workbook) would provide the causal attribution rules and the policy template, but would not frame the problem as a 'governance-by-half' failure or explain why the conceptual mechanism (control loop) appeared to be working while the operational layer was absent.

tests.prompt: Our service has been at 99.995% availability for a year against a 99.9% SLO. We've never declared a freeze. Our error budget policy has a freeze trigger and causal attribution rules — everything looks correct on paper. We just noticed that consumer teams have removed all their retry logic because 'your service never goes down.' Governance is technically working but something seems wrong. What is it?
tests.merged_advantage: The merged skill identifies the overachievement failure mode — the Chubby pattern — which the SRE book's conceptual framework contributes. A policy that only triggers on exhaustion will never fire when the budget is never consumed. The merged skill prescribes adding an overachievement clause to the governance policy: if the budget is never consumed, schedule controlled degradation. This connects the SRE book's Chubby warning (source 1) directly to the policy template mechanics (source 2) as a mandatory policy component.
tests.source1_gap: error-budget-conflict-resolution (SRE book) provides the Chubby overachievement warning, but frames it as a general principle rather than a specific governance policy component. It would diagnose the consumer dependency problem but not specify how to encode the fix into the written policy framework.
tests.source2_gap: error-budget-policy-framework (Workbook) provides the policy template structure but focuses on exhaustion triggers and causal attribution. The overachievement case (budget never consumed) is not addressed by the Workbook's policy template. It would not prompt the team to add a controlled degradation clause.

tests.prompt: We want to implement error budget governance from scratch. We have an SLO but no policy and no tri-party agreement. Our senior SRE wants to follow the SRE book approach; our platform lead read the SRE Workbook and says we need a written policy first. They've been arguing for two weeks. Who's right and how do we do both?
tests.merged_advantage: The merged skill resolves the false conflict: both are right at different layers. The SRE book's conceptual mechanism (control loop, self-policing, joint SLO) must be understood first — without understanding why error budgets work, the written policy is cargo-culted process. But the Workbook's written policy, causal attribution, and tri-party ratification are the operational layer that makes the mechanism function. The merged skill provides the correct sequence: establish the mechanism and management mandate (SRE book), then operationalize with the written policy and ratification (Workbook). Neither source alone explains why both are necessary.
tests.source1_gap: error-budget-conflict-resolution (SRE book) provides the mechanism and the conceptual case for why it works, but does not provide the written policy template, causal attribution rules, or tri-party ratification requirement — leaving the platform lead's concern entirely unanswered.
tests.source2_gap: error-budget-policy-framework (Workbook) provides the written policy template and ratification process, but assumes the conceptual mechanism is already understood. Without the SRE book framing, teams implement the policy template as documentation overhead without internalizing why the freeze is a legitimate mechanism rather than SRE power-play.
